old_claim.note: Early Electrical Appliances is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because electric fans are themselves an early appliance category, while the source-backed mechanism is the electric motor driving fan blades.

source_shape.source_locator: History and motor sections identify Schuyler Wheeler's 1886 fan and explain that a DC or AC motor drives the rotating fan shaft.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source explains electric fans through motor-driven blades and early fan designs, not through a prior broad electrical-appliance category.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because electrical appliances are a taxonomy that includes electric fans, keeping that category as a predecessor launders classification context into a causal edge.

ontology_before: Electric Fans depended on Early Electrical Appliances, implying the broad appliance category historically preceded the motor-driven fan device.
ontology_after: Electric Fans depends on Electric Motor; early appliances remain a neighboring category rather than a prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electric_fans no longer directly depends on electrical_appliances_early.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to mass-market household fan adoption after appliance markets were already established.
A stronger source shows electric fans were developed from a specific prior appliance platform rather than from motor-driven fan design.
A separate electrical_appliance_marketplaces node is added and explicitly scopes commercialization rather than invention.
short_reason: Early appliances are a category, not the causal predecessor.
